source_display: AI as Brain Crutches for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching

source_display: Death by a Thousand Cuts (Pilot Trap)
evidence: Vrijen warned against the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is
Chris is building an ADHD support product called '360.' He's been building his edtech product for almost a year
Chris is Heather's colleague at their coaching practice - V currently receiving executive function coaching from Heather
He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly
Chris revealed having 65,000 active email subscribers built over years—a significant distribution asset
Vrijen emphasized that founder-led sales provides customer research, network building, and messaging refinement—critical activities for early-stage companies
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies

source_display: Jobs Are Getting Paid to Learn
evidence: He explained that jobs are essentially 'getting paid to learn,' but gatekeeping makes experience inaccessible

source_display: Neopet Monetization Model for AI Agents
evidence: Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing
Careerspan pivoting to generate revenue through India recruiting partnership before targeting mid-year acquisition... team reduced to three people, with two founders unpaid
visible links between individual work and the product/service being sold are increasingly essential in 2026... engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable
these roles exist primarily to organize attention but don't add direct value... these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape, with many PM positions facing similar elimination

his product creates portfolios first and assessments second, acknowledging that assessments are difficult to execute effectively. He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value
Maximilian shared that he's preparing to launch his new product in approximately one month
He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs. He outlined three strategic levels
Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems
Threshold rubrics: Ask the AI to create an evaluation rubric, fill it out, assign scores, and iterate output until achieving the highest level
Offensive and defensive prompting: Expansive generation (expand option set) vs. critical tightening (attack, break down, shore up)
Only 3,300 LinkedIn connections versus 45,000 imported contacts from a 2015 data import, revealing two largely disjoint professional networks
Social capital proxy: Inbound versus outbound connection ratio as a measure of professional standing
Documented 1,800% increase in inbound connections when increasing posting frequency, strong evidence for visibility strategies

source_display: Unhobbling AI with Its Own Home
evidence: Ben highlighted Zo's integration capabilities... He emphasized the importance of intentional prompting—documenting AI conversations in plan files... embrace the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team

source_display: Plan Files as Future of Coding
evidence: He positioned this as the future of coding: planning systems through narrative structure rather than pure code alone, with Zo planning enhanced UI for long-range task planning

He shared his metacognitive patterns for working effectively with AI: narrative before data, use yourself as a guinea pig, never accept the first answer, consistently ask 'what else?' and 'delight me'
Ben Guo introduced Zo Computer as a managed version of Claude Code, giving the AI its own computer (managed cloud server)
Ben Guo (co-founder of Zo Computer and Vrijen as a power Zo user)
